summary:
Oda Nobunaga (1534–1582 AD) was the first and most ruthless of the three warlords who reunified Japan after a century of the Sengoku period ('Warring States'), transforming a feudal patchwork of competing daimyo domains into a centralizing state through military innovation, administrative pragmatism, and spectacular violence. Nobunaga rose from a minor domain in Owari province through his mastery of firearms — particularly his tactical use of volley fire by ashigaru (foot soldiers) with arquebuses at the [[event:2421|Battle of Nagashino]] (1575), which destroyed the cavalry of the Takeda clan (whose legendary mounted samurai had been considered invincible) and demonstrated that the age of individual samurai heroism had ended. By the time of his death, Nobunaga controlled perhaps a third of Japan's territory.

Nobunaga's methods were as innovative as they were brutal. He destroyed the power of Buddhist warrior monks — particularly the Ikko-ikki (militant Pure Land Buddhist leagues) and the Enryakuji monastery on Mount Hiei — with comprehensive violence, reportedly killing thousands of monks, women, and children. He welcomed [[event:2489|Portuguese]] merchants and missionaries, viewing Christianity as a useful counterweight to Buddhist institutional power and imported firearms as a military asset. He systematically abolished internal toll barriers (rakuichi rakuza) that had restricted commerce, stimulating economic growth. His assassination in 1582 at the Honnō-ji temple by the treachery of his general Akechi Mitsuhide cut short his unification before completion — but his successors [[event:3237|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] and [[event:3308|Tokugawa Ieyasu]] built directly on his foundations.
